did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an AI tool trained on dermoscopic images to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and identify dermoscopic criteria. The study compared the performance of this AI model when trained with labels from a single dermatologist versus a consensus-based standard reference inferred using Expectation Maximization.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details specific Materials and Methods (collecting 1230 dermoscopic images, training an AI model, building a standard reference using Expectation Maximization), Results (Kappa = 0.9079, PPV = 0.9670, statistical comparisons using McNemar’s test), and a Conclusion based on new data analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Concordance in Basal Cell Carcinoma Diagnosis. Building a Proper Standard Reference to Train Artificial Intelligence Tools
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of establishing reliable standard references for training artificial intelligence tools in basal cell carcinoma (BCC) diagnosis, where ground truth is subjective. The researchers utilized a dataset of 1230 dermoscopic images from primary health centers to train an AI model, comparing performance when labeled by a single dermatologist versus a consensus-based standard inferred via Expectation Maximization from four dermatologists. Inter-rater agreement among the dermatologists was high for BCC classification (Kappa = 0.9079) but lower for specific dermoscopic criteria. The AI tool's performance was evaluated using McNemar’s test and Hamming distance, revealing statistical differences between models trained on individual versus consensus labels. The prospective test set of 204 images demonstrated that deriving a standard reference from multiple experts mitigates individual bias and enhances AI interpretability, which is crucial for clinical adoption.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary diagnostic target; 588 images in training set and 100 in test set exhibited BCC patterns; 88 lesions confirmed BCC via biopsy in prospective set.
Negative class for binary classification; 642 images in training set and 104 in test set were non-BCC; 3 lesions confirmed non-BCC via biopsy in prospective set.
Identified as a negative criterion for BCC; appeared in 577 training images and 16 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 72 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 63 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 33 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 28 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 12 prospective test images.
BCC dermoscopic criterion; appeared in 102 prospective test images.
65 images in the training dataset lacked any identifiable BCC patterns.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus non-BCC
all_task_types.details: The AI model performs binary classification to categorize dermoscopic images as BCC or non-BCC. The study reports 96.7% classification accuracy for this task on the prospective test set. The model was trained using labels from a single dermatologist versus a consensus-based standard reference inferred via Expectation Maximization.

all_task_types.target: Identification of specific BCC dermoscopic criteria (binary presence/absence)
all_task_types.details: The AI model also performs multi-label binary classification for seven specific dermoscopic patterns (Pigment Network, Ulceration, Blue-Gray Ovoid Nests, Multiple Blue-Gray Globules, Maple Leaf-Like Areas, Spoke-Wheel Structures, Arborizing Telangiectasia). The study reports 82% accuracy for this pattern identification task. Annotations are encoded as binary vectors (1/0) indicating presence or absence of each criterion.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective Teledermatology Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 1230 dermoscopic images (256x256 pixels) sourced from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena in Seville, Spain, and its associated primary healthcare facilities. Images were collected retrospectively via teledermatology between 2019 and 2021. The dataset was annotated by four dermatologists for BCC diagnosis and seven dermoscopic criteria.

use.training: Used as the primary training foundation for the AI system. Labels were derived from a single dermatologist and a consensus-based standard reference inferred via Expectation Maximization.
use.other: Used to build the standard reference (SR) using Expectation Maximization on diagnoses from four dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Test Dataset
datasets_used.description: A prospective collection of 204 dermoscopic images (256x256 pixels) acquired at Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena in Seville, Spain. This dataset was compiled after the development of the AI model to analyze inter-observer concordance and evaluate AI performance. It includes histopathological validation for a subset of lesions.

use.testing: Used to prospectively test the AI tool's performance (accuracy, PPV) and analyze inter-observer concordance among different standard references.
use.other: Used for histopathological validation (biopsy) of 91 lesions to establish ground truth for clinical assessment correlation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate pairwise agreement among dermatologists for classifying lesions as BCC or non-BCC and for assessing concordance on specific dermoscopic patterns.
To assess overall concordance among all four raters for BCC classification and for specific dermoscopic criteria.
To assess the statistical significance of differences in performance between two paired AI systems (trained on individual vs. consensus labels) by quantifying discordant predictions.
To assess overall agreement across all BCC features simultaneously between pairs of dermatologists and between different standard references (SRs).
To statistically infer a consensus-based standard reference from multiple annotators' diagnoses.
To infer the standard reference by reflecting the majority opinion among raters.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance (equivalence) between the two paired AI systems (one trained on individual dermatologist labels and the other on consensus-based standard reference labels).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between the two paired AI systems.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used McNemar's test to assess statistical significance of differences in performance between AI models trained with individual versus consensus labels. For the 'Blue-Gray Ovoid Nests' pattern, the p-value was 0.0003, leading to the rejection of the null hypothesis of equivalence. For other patterns (Pigment Network, Ulceration, Multiple Blue-Gray Globules, Maple Leaf-Like Areas, Spoke-Wheel Structures, Arborizing Telangiectasia), p-values were 0.7893, 0.2636, 0.1336, 0.5050, 0.6171, and 0.0662 respectively, indicating no statistically significant difference at the 0.05 threshold for those specific criteria.

Retrospective_Teledermatology_Dataset.description: Training dataset comprising 1230 dermoscopic images collected retrospectively (2019-2021) from Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena and primary healthcare facilities.
Retrospective_Teledermatology_Dataset.notes: Inter-rater agreement among four dermatologists was high for BCC vs non-BCC classification. Fleiss' Kappa of 0.9079 indicates nearly perfect agreement. Cohen's Kappa pairwise mean was 0.9075. PPV of 0.9670 was calculated based on biopsy-confirmed cases in the prospective set, but reported in the context of overall diagnostic concordance.

Prospective_Test_Dataset.description: Test dataset comprising 204 dermoscopic images acquired prospectively at Hospital Universitario Virgen Macarena. 91 lesions underwent biopsy (88 BCC, 3 non-BCC).

AI_System_1.Performance_Notes: Used as baseline for comparison against AI System 2.

AI_System_2.Training_Label_Source: Consensus Standard Reference inferred via Expectation Maximization (EM)
AI_System_2.Performance_Notes: Compared against AI System 1 using McNemar's test and Hamming distance.

Hamming_Distance_AI_System_Comparison.Description: Comparison of predictions between AI System 1 (D1 labels) and AI System 2 (EM labels) on the 204-image test set.

Prospective_Test_Dataset.notes: Statistical differences were found in AI model performance for Blue-Gray Ovoid Nests (p=0.0003). Agreement between AI systems was lowest for Blue-Gray Ovoid Nests and Arborizing Telangiectasia. Hamming distance between derived SRs (MV and EM) was significantly lower than between individual dermatologists, indicating reduced disagreement.

architecture_used.main_model: Triple classification architecture combining CNNs and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)

architecture_details.description: A deep learning approach enriched with color science insights. The architecture processes three types of inputs: original dermoscopic images, color-quantized versions in perceptual color spaces, and hand-crafted perceptually correlated color-texture descriptors. The image data is processed by Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), while the texture features are fed into a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). All outputs are fused for final classification.
architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image processing; Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) for texture features
input_modalities: Color-quantized versions in perceptual color spaces

training_details.loss_function: Weighted focal loss (class weights adjusted according to estimated frequency of each dermoscopic pattern to mitigate class imbalance)
standard_references_used: Consensus-based standard reference inferred via Expectation Maximization (AI System 2)

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (BCC vs. non-BCC) and multi-label binary classification for 7 dermoscopic criteria
classification_or_output_head.fusion_method: Fusion of outputs from CNNs and MLP

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AI model trained with labels from Dermatologist 1 (AI System 1)
AI model trained with consensus-based standard reference inferred via Expectation Maximization (AI System 2)
Triple classification architecture combining CNNs and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)
Unsupervised deep learning methodology employing sparse autoencoders (referenced as prior work by Kharazmi et al.)
AI tool from Serrano et al. (referenced as prior work)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: 90°, 180°, and 270° rotations; horizontal flips; vertical flips
Input resizing: Images standardized to 256x256 pixels
Color quantization: Conversion of original dermoscopic images to color-quantized versions in perceptual color spaces
Feature extraction: Generation of hand-crafted perceptually correlated color-texture descriptors

data_augmentation_techniques.details: 90°, 180°, and 270° rotations were applied to the training images.
Horizontal and vertical flips were applied to the training images.
The original training set of 1230 images was increased to 4920 images through these augmentation techniques.

Data augmentation was explicitly not used for class balancing to avoid mismatch between training and deployment distributions. Instead, class imbalance was addressed using a weighted focal loss function with class weights adjusted according to the estimated frequency of each dermoscopic pattern.

conclusions: Deriving a standard reference (SR) from multiple expert opinions mitigates individual bias and enhances AI interpretability, which is key for clinical adoption.
Agreement among dermatologists was high for BCC versus non-BCC classification (Kappa = 0.9079; PPV = 0.9670) but lower for specific dermoscopic criteria.
Statistical differences were found in the performance of AI models trained with individual dermatologist labels versus those trained with consensus-based standard reference labels.
important_remarks: The study addresses the fundamental limitation that no established ground truth exists for dermoscopic patterns, necessitating the use of inferred standard references (via Expectation Maximization or Majority Voting) rather than biopsy confirmation for pattern-level labels.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, particularly for rare dermoscopic patterns (e.g., spoke-wheel pattern appeared in only 12 out of 204 prospective test images), which may affect the reliability of performance metrics for these specific criteria.
While inter-rater agreement for BCC diagnosis was high (Fleiss' Kappa = 0.9079), agreement for specific dermoscopic criteria was lower, highlighting the subjectivity inherent in pattern annotation.

The study utilized a retrospective training set (1230 images) and a separate prospective test set (204 images) collected at a later time, which helps mitigate data leakage risks but limits the sample size for prospective validation.

Histopathological validation was available for only 91 lesions in the prospective set (88 BCC, 3 non-BCC), meaning the majority of the test set relied on clinical follow-up or expert consensus rather than definitive biopsy confirmation.
Data augmentation was explicitly not used for class balancing to avoid mismatch between training and deployment distributions; instead, class imbalance was addressed using weighted focal loss.

The AI model's performance was sensitive to the choice of standard reference, with statistically significant differences found in the detection of specific patterns (e.g., Blue-Gray Ovoid Nests, p=0.0003) when trained on individual vs. consensus labels.

The study population is limited to images from primary health centers in Seville, Spain, sent via teledermatology, which may introduce geographic and equipment-specific biases limiting generalizability to other clinical settings.
